speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking with Dr. Emil Arnold. I'm Leslie [? [INAUDIBLE]. ?] Dr. Arnold, you were describing your hideout. Was that in Lodz or outside of Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just outside the town. It-- I think it was still within the geographical uh, town limits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Lodz is in northeastern Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz is in uh, middle eastern part of Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe further the family that took care of you, if each member of the family knew that they were hiding a mother and two children? And also, the hideout itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was a uh, point of concern. There were two children, grown-- grown children. Their daughter was 17, Ella. And Milos was 19, uh, the son. Uh-- I guess they knew how to keep a secret. However, the son was engaged to a neighbor's daughter. And he told her about it. The parents were very upset. If they got-- If we got caught, they would all get shot, the children, the parents, everybody. And they knew about it. So I guess he must have been very trusting to tell his fiancee about it. And she insisted she wanted to meet us. So he brought her one evening and we had a conversation. And she said that her parents actually suspected that something was happening. I-- I-- they-- I-- you know, they were neighbors. And I guess they could tell that something was going on. I guess they could see in the evening, the woman coming to the barn, carrying things and so on. So they said they-- they suspected. But uh, they kept quiet. And nobody knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in that barn for a year and a half. I-- it-- it's-- it seems strange. I remember very little, except for isolated incidents, what actually happened. A year and a half, lying and sitting inside a uh, small c-- carved-out room, uh, seems like a very old ti-- uh, long time. And just what do you do, all this time? And I don't really remember. I remember isolated incidents. I remember that we did a lot of talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was educated in the arts and literature. So she would uh, talk about uh-- uh, she studied Greek and Latin, uh, when she went to school. She-- I remember she said she read the and the in the original Greek.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she would tell us the stories of Greek mythology and Roman history. And-- and-- and-- and uh, Carthage Wars and uh, all the Polish literature and German literature. So uh, it was kind of educational. We were lying there, uh, asleep. Did you have any materials that she could teach you with? Or was it mostly listening?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dark. We-- we had no light there. So uh, when we came out, it was night. So there was no light uh, during the night. There was no light during the day, no. So there were no materials. So at night they would bring us food. And uh, I don't remember ever being hungry. So I guess we had enough to eat. They would bring us bread and meat and molasses. They-- they had a farm. It was not a large farm, but they had several cows and pigs. And they grew vegetables and potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had enough produce and food for themselves and for us. And they sold the rest. So that's how they got their clothing and other things that they needed. So they were pretty much self-sufficient. Do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they shared it with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if your parents paid them anything, eh-- for them to take care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, as far as I know, there was no mention of any money, of any uh, rewards for them, uh, during-- during the time. I'm pretty sure they didn't do it for any money. I know that when it was all over, we had uh, some gold coins, which was the only valuable currency around. The popular unit of currency was the old Russian-- czarist Russian five-ruble gold piece. It was about the si-- the size of a dime, or slightly bigger. Very pretty. And uh, you could buy a lot of food and other things with it. So we had a number of those sewn into our clothing. And aft-- after it was over, I know mother gave them-- I don't think she gave them anything during the hiding. But later on we took the stuff out and she gave them a few pieces. And I don't know that they wanted anything, really. So they definitely didn't do it for money. Something like this, you don't do for money. In fact, to this day, I don't know why they did this. I don't know why anybody would risk their life to-- to hide people like this for a year and a half. It was very, very, uh, risky for-- to do. And I don't understand why they did this. I know a [? nu-- ?] number of people uh, took tremendous risk, risked their lives and their families' lives. So I don't know why they did it. I guess they just felt that this was the right thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in this haystack, like this, day after day. I only remember isolated incidents. One thing I-- I can't forget is the-- there must have been a dead mouse or a rat somewhere inside that haystack. And for two or more months, we just had to live with this terrible smell. I can still smell it now. Finally, it was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another incident I remember was uh, Germans were either having a maneuvers exercises, or maybe they suspected something. There were a number of Germans walking around the farm with dogs. And I remember dog or dogs outside of the haystack, barking. And the soldier, he w-- walked over. And we could hear him poking his bayonet into the straw a number of times. But of course, it was thick enough so it couldn't penetrate. So finally they gave up and walked away. But that was a rather scary time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what he was saying, if anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember what he was saying. I just-- we couldn't really hear very much. We could hear the dogs barking. We heard them shouting. And uh-- and I don't know whether they suspected anything. We thought that-- that they must have found out that we were here. Uh, later on, when we talked to our hosts, it seems that they-- they were just there for another reason. They were not looking for us specifically. But they did poke with their bayonets into-- into the-- into the straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you recall if your mother kept-- kept tally of the dates? She knew when Jewish holidays occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any celebration of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't know the-- no. She didn't know the Jewish holidays. I-- I think we knew roughly, what date it was. Perhaps not exactly. But we knew roughly what day it was. And uh-- [PAUSES] The following-- the following summer the straw had to be changed. Because these things sort of get rotted away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the time when we uh, saw daylight for-- for a couple of days. We went back on top of the haystack inside the barn while he took off the old haystack and put in a new one and made a new room for us. During that time they brought us some papers. So we could read the papers. And there were rumors going around. We could uh-- we could tell. And it-- it was known the Germans, after a certain period of time, were not doing so well. So we knew about Stalingrad, for example, when it happened. Didn't know exactly what happened. But we knew that the Germans were not very successful there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a time when really one could hope that uh, sooner or later, uh, they would lose. So I imagine that that was one reason why they just persisted for such a long time, they-- keeping-- keeping us there. Because they expected that the war would be over and the Germans would be gone. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name the lady of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her first name I don't remember. I just-- I try to remember. Uh, uh, I don't remember her first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have conversations with either the master of the house or Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]? Do you-- were there conversations? Or did they just bring the food and leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, they-- they-- they stayed and talked. Uh, I don't remember any specific conversations. But they would tell us about the rumors. They would tell us if there was uh, an-- any Jews were caught. Periodically that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: People that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Word got around that they knew. No, nobody we knew specifically. Mother tried to find out what happened to our father. And I guess that they tried to investigate. Uh, nobody knew. Uh, then there were rumors about how the war was going, what was happening on the Western Front, what was happening on the Russian front. So we talk about these things. Not very long, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you warm enough?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't remember being very cold. I guess straw is a pretty good insulation. We had this very thick feather quilt on top. Winters are very cold, but we never really felt cold in the winter or hot in the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So other than just having to sit and lie all day, uh, it was not terribly uncomfortable. Yeah but as I said before, it's hard to imagine that we stayed there for a year and a half. I just uh, can't keep track of it day by day. But somehow, days-- the days went by.

speaker: interviewee
text: And uh, then the rumors became stronger and stronger. We knew that the Russians were advancing. So they would tell us all that. And uh, one day we started hearing gunfire. So we knew that time was close. And gunfire got closer and closer. Then it was quiet. The following morning I went out and looked through the crack in the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw Russian soldiers sitting in a ditch with a machine gun. So I called to my mother. I said, do you want to see the Russians outside? So we very cautiously came out. And sure enough, the Russians are there. [? Saturday. ?] What was that date? Do you recall the date, or the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was February of 1944. I don't remember exact-- the exact date. But I think it was very early February, February second or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: C--Could you describe that more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so the Russians were there. And uh, we went out. And that was the first time we started to think what to do with ourselves. You know? Saw the daylight. It was a very cold morning, I remember. So we decide that well, time for us to uh, start fending for ourselves. My mother managed to find an apartment uh, not too far away. So we moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same town, not very far away, in Lodz. So we stayed there for a while. We still visited with the [NON-ENGLISH] occasionally. But this didn't last very long, only a few days. And then Germans started advancing again and the Russians started retreating. So there was a uh, big panic. And uh, all the Jews-- well, there were not very many Jews left. The original population-- Jewish population was something like 20,000 in Lodz. I understand there were 150 people left. None of those 150 were any people that we had known. A-- A-- Anybody that I knew uh, wasn't there anymore. Mother tried to find out what happened to father. There were rumors. They said they saw them in the woods. They saw him here. They saw him there. They thought he acce-- escaped with the partisans in the-- in the forest. But uh, there was no confirmation. And uh, just to this day, I don't know what happened to him. Our cousin [NON-ENGLISH] had Christian papers. And she did survive. She was in Germany for a while and she finally wound up in the-- in the US.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after those first few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: So af-- so after those first few days, people started retreating. So we took whatever we had and started walking east together with the Russians. [? So that's how we-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't have very much. So-- [LAUGHS] --at least we didn't have any heavy baggage to carry. So we just started walking, essentially, just a, uh, a day's food and some bread, and not much clothing. So we started walking back. After awhile, we got a ride with some Russian soldiers on the back of the truck. And we wound up in [NON-ENGLISH], which is the next town to the east, about 60 miles away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, were-- were you carrying any family documents? Any--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had my father's diploma. I guess we still had it. Or perhaps it was-- or perhaps we left it on the farm. I don't remember exactly. But-- but my father had a diploma with him when we left the ghetto. And uh, I still have it. So I don't think I carried it-- we carr-- we carried it with us then. So it must have stayed on the farm and then we retrieved it later when we came back. But we stayed in [NON-ENGLISH]. Germans didn't-- didn't occupy Lodz. They got close. But they never actually got in-- into town. But nobody want to take any chances. So we stayed, found a place to live in [NON-ENGLISH] for several months, three months, something like that. Or two months, perhaps it was only one month, March or April, I guess. We came back to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well how did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Uh, as I said before, never all this time did we feel hungry. So I guess food was available uh, somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's about uh, 60 miles east from Lodz. I remember going to school there. I uh-- don't remember what grade it was. But I remember going to school for about a month or so. Eventually we went back. We still had the same apartment that we originally had in uh-- af-- after we were liberated. I never saw our original apartment again that I can remember, where my father uh, l--lived with us. I don't remember ever seeing it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you lived in [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back we-- we-- we had-- uh, we went back to this little apartment near the place where we were hiding. And after about a month, I just felt so confined. I uh, I just couldn't take it anymore. I can't quite explain it. But we had a year and a half of togetherness. And I just couldn't take it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran away from house-- from the house. And uh, I decided to join the army. I uh, got a ri-- hitched a ride to an Army base. It was a Polish army unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Polish army organized inside Russia. Uh, the people in-- in this army were-- were Poles who were originally, most of them, expelled from Poland by the Russians. Uh, Russians knew they were going to retake Poland. And they wanted to make sure that the military and the government was communist-oriented rather than right-wing-oriented. So they wanted to make sure that the Polish army was Russian sponsored and not otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they formed a Russian arm-- a Polish army under Russian auspices. The uniforms were Polish. Uh, many officers were Polish. Uh, but also, many were Russian. Because I guess there were not enough qualified people [INAUDIBLE]-- Did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they did not know that I was Jewish. Um, I felt much more anti-Semitism after the liberation than any time, uh-- well, of course, I can't speak about German occupation. But before the war, uh, in comparison, uh, it was very uh, mild. I remember after we were liberated in Lodz, I was walking on the street. That was short time after the liberation, perhaps uh-- or perhaps it was after we came back from [NON-ENGLISH]. So must have been about two months after the liberation. I was walking down the street and I was attacked by a-- a bunch of kids. Older-- most of them were older than me, perhaps a year, two years, three years older, teenagers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Several of them, saying oh, you're a Jew. What are you doing here? You're not supposed to be alive. You know? And they beat me up. So that's something I couldn't understand, just why-- why would they do that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 12, 12 and a half. So uh, when I tried to join the army, first of all, I told them I was an orphan. I had no place to go. My family was killed during the war. And I wanted to join the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were there, had you said goodbye to your mother or just ran away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I ran away. She didn't know where I was. They didn't ask me whether I was Jewish or not. But later on, I pretended I was-- I was a Roman Catholic, which was, uh, I guess, tricky when you're in the army to-- [LAUGHS] --to be able to pretend for-- for some time. But I-- I managed somehow. Anyway, so I-- I said I wanted to-- to-- to join the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the Polish army. So they took me to the commanding officer. He spoke to his commanding officer. They finally decided all right, let the kid stay. So they put me in a-- um-- it was a reconnaissance battalion. They put me in the communications unit, which was the radio unit. So they thought it was a good thing for me. I could learn how to operate the radio and learn the Morse code. I thought it was very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that particular unit, it was a small unit. Most of the people were actually Russians. They wore Polish uniforms but they were Russians, because of a specialist in radio communication in Morse code. So most of my friends at that time were Russians. And everybody spoke Russian, even though it was for a Polish army. There was a Polish teacher who taught in Polish because they were supposed to learn Polish. After all, they were Polish soldiers. But at least half of them were-- were Russian. Uh, I wrote a letter to my mother and I said, I'm OK. I'm in the uh-- in the army. I'd like to stay here in the army. And this was during the war, of course. So when you send a letter, they-- they-- there was no-- no return address on it. And I imagine that this was secret enough so she wouldn't find out where I-- where I am. Well, a couple of weeks later, I just go out, and there she is, with a-- walking with the Polish colonel down the path. I guess she met somebody who happened to be Jew-- a Jew, a colonel in the Polish army. And he uh-- they were friends during that time. And he managed to locate me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So uh, I don't-- I don't know how or why she agreed that I would stay in the army. But she let me stay. And they-- she didn't object.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess she knew that-- that-- that I was stubborn enough. If I wanted to run away, I'd run away. And this way, they assured her-- they assured her, I guess, that I was in really no immediate danger. I was not on the front lines. And in fact, this was true. The-- the Russians, I suppose, wanted to make sure that the Polish army would not get decimated during the war. So they made sure that they were really not included in the very heavy fighting. So most of them would survive when-- when the war was over. And that's-- that was the-- that was the case. I stayed in the army. They got a tailor, made me a uniform that fit and got me a pair of boots that fit. And uh, I actually performed the duties of a soldier. I was promoted to corporal eventually, and learned the Morse code. I uh, took and sent messages, so on. And after a month or so, the unit moved out, started going west, following the front by about 20, 30 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wind out-- uh, wound up uh, by Warsaw. We stayed on the other side of the river while Warsaw was besieged. There is a long story about what happened in Warsaw. Nobody could understand why didn't we just go in and take Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it so happened the Polish underground was very right-wing-oriented. And the Russians wanted to make sure that none of these people survived. So they just waited until the Germans killed all of them. And then they finally took Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So uh, we were sitting there for several months, doing nothing, just perhaps shooting rifles across the river, just killing time. Eventually we went to Warsaw, took Warsaw, and started going on. Went to Germany. I wound up in Berlin about one or two days after Berlin was taken. Stayed in Berlin. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was uh, May, I believe, in 1945. So then the war was over. We went back and uh, we stationed near Warsaw for a short time. And then I was discharged. I had enough army life, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they let me go. After all, I [? was ?] underage. So uh, they [? sent ?] a document, being discharged to uh, continue his studies. You said that you-- you were in charge of the radio and you learned the Morse code?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't in charge, but uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Participated? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you witness any bloodshed?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit, yes. There were two cases in our unit where people got badly wounded. And uh, it just so happened, I guess, during battle, you can't really predict what's going to be where. The-- the-- on those two occasions, Germans had an ambush and uh-- and they-- they-- one person was shot in the head. He was very close to me, to-- physically. So I saw that happen. And uh, and another case, I don't remember exactly how it happened. but-- but most of the time we were not really-- we were not fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they gathering other Poles to be part of their army?

speaker: interviewee
text: N--not that I know of. I don't think that uh, there was any-- any recruitment during the uh-- during uh-- during the time that-- that they were advancing to-- to the west. Perhaps there was, but I-- I-- I haven't seen any cases like this. The Poles were actually-- actually, it was a very clever strategy. Because I remember that the Poles were very happy to see us. They-- Poles and Russians don't get together very-- along very well. And uh, all the Poles that-- that we met as we were advancing west were very surprised and happy to see that there was a Polish army. So uh, it was a very clever move on the part of the Russians to-- well, it just help to establish a communist government in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the-- in the meantime, my mother and sister had moved from Lodz. They went west and settled in Lodz, which is the western part of Poland. So I joined them there. I had an apartment. I started going to high school. My mother managed to contact her sister in New York. How did you find each other? How'd you reunite with your mother and sister? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I knew where they were, of course, because uh, we corresponded by-- by mail. So I knew where they were. And they knew where I was. Well, they-- they had my military address. So they could write to me. So I uh-- I-- I-- I remember I came on leave for about a day or so or two. And finally I was discharged. I went to school for some-- for a brief period of time. Uh, and my mother contacted her sister in New York. And she started-- uh-- um-- to make a-- to try to bring her-- us to uh, to the States, which was not easy. Nobody could leave Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, the-- it was decided the only way to get out was to illegally leave Poland, go to Germany, the occupied of Germany-- part of Germany. And uh--
